epigraph.text: Judge not, that you be not judged.
epigraph.quote: Multiple comparisons inflate the Type I error rate unless properly corrected.

content.text:
When ANOVA rejects $H_0$, we know at least one mean differs—but 
which ones? Multiple comparison procedures identify specific differences.

With $k$ groups, there are $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise comparisons. 
If each is tested at $\alpha = 0.05$, the probability of at least 
one Type I error (familywise error rate) grows quickly.

For independent tests:
\[
 P(\text{at least one Type I error}) = 1 - (1-\alpha)^m
\]

content.title: Bonferroni Correction
content.content:
To maintain familywise error rate $\alpha$ across $m$ comparisons, 
test each at level $\alpha/m$.

content.text: Simple but conservative (may miss real differences).

content.title: Tukey's HSD
content.content:
For equal sample sizes, two means differ significantly if:
\[
 |\bar{x}_i - \bar{x}_j| > q_{\alpha,k,df_W} \cdot \sqrt{MSW/n}
\]
where $q$ is the Studentized range critical value.

content.text:
Tukey's method is exact and less conservative than Bonferroni for 
all pairwise comparisons.

items: **Dunnett's test:** For comparing to a control group
**Fisher's LSD:** Only if ANOVA is significant
